Detroit wellness resource hub

Food support, movement, and mental wellness in one place.

Run 4 Your Mind connects families, students, and community partners with local food assistance, neighborhood recreation, school-ready workshops, and healthy habits that make care easier to find.

Local Food + Wellness Finder

Source-backed data
Uses local source-backed data first, then checks for a daily hosted update.

Resource cards

Quick paths to food, recreation, and support.

Browse by need, age group, and neighborhood type. These cards can link to live partner pages when your final directory is ready.

Weekly goals + videos

Build one healthy habit and learn what support exists by state.

Use the weekly goal tracker for simple momentum, then browse your YouTube resource library by state topic.

This week's goal

Move for 20 minutes after school or dinner.

Check off each day you complete the habit. Progress saves while this page is open.

4 of 7 days complete

Community stories

Role models showing what care looks like in Detroit.

Spotlight students, educators, coaches, organizers, parents, and neighbors who make healthy choices feel possible.

Nominate Someone
“

Maya, youth run captain

Started a lunch-hour walking group that helped classmates reset before afternoon classes.

“

Coach Andre, mentor

Connects families with pantry hours before practice so students can focus and participate.

“

Ms. Rivera, school counselor

Uses weekly goal cards to help students name stress, movement, rest, and support.

School workshops

Ready-to-implement lessons for food access, movement, and mental wellness.

Each workshop can be delivered by Run 4 Your Mind or adapted by school staff with a simple kit.

Plan a Workshop
Grades 5-8

Fuel, Mood, and Movement

Students connect meals, hydration, movement, and stress in a practical classroom session.

  • 45-minute lesson plan
  • Family resource handout
  • One-week goal tracker
Request this module
High school

Finding Help Without Shame

Students learn how to search for food assistance, ask for support, and share resources with peers.

  • Resource finder activity
  • Role-play discussion cards
  • Local partner list
Ask about availability
Staff + families

Community Wellness Night

A school event format with pantry partners, movement demos, and healthy habit stations.

  • Event station guide
  • Partner outreach template
  • Follow-up email copy
Build an event

About Run 4 Your Mind

Founded in 2022 to make brain health practical, active, and community-centered.

Run 4 Your Mind grew from Alam’s personal experience with running, research, and community outreach.

Alam’s Story

Alam founded Run 4 Your Mind in 2022 after seeing how movement, education, and early health awareness could change the way young people think about brain health. His experience running cross country helped him build discipline, confidence, and a healthier relationship with his own wellbeing.

Through research exposure in a Wayne State pharmacology lab and continued advocacy around brain health, Alam saw a way to connect science with everyday habits. Run 4 Your Mind brings that message to students and families: small, consistent steps like running, walking, eating well, and asking for support can protect the mind and strengthen the whole person.

Now, the goal is to bring that impact to Detroit by connecting schools, families, and neighborhoods with food resources, wellness education, movement opportunities, and role models who make health feel possible.

Cross country experience
Running became a foundation for improving physical health, focus, resilience, and mental wellbeing.
Science-to-community mission
Lab exposure helped shape a nonprofit focused on accessible brain health education.
Detroit impact
The organization aims to support students and families through movement, food access, and school workshops.
Wayne State University School of Medicine

Experience in Pharmacology lab inspires high schooler to start nonprofit

Coverage of Alam’s research experience and the inspiration behind launching Run 4 Your Mind.

Meet the Farmington Hills high schooler changing the game in brain health
